title: Supporting the designation of May 16, 2025, as "Endangered Species Day".
plain_summary: This bill supports making May 16, 2025 a special day called Endangered Species Day. The day would celebrate how well the Endangered Species Act has worked to save plants and animals from dying out completely. The bill points out that more than two thousand species are still in danger. However, it also shows that conservation efforts have been very successful, saving ninety-nine percent of protected species from extinction.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.

sections.title: Resolution Supporting the designation of May 16, 2025, as Endangered Species Day
sections.text: Plants and animals provide countless benefits to people, including helping our health, culture, economy, recreation, and spiritual well-being. In the United States and around the world, more than two thousand three hundred species are recognized as being at risk of dying out completely now or in the future. Many more species are seeing their numbers drop and their chances of survival get worse. The planet is going through a crisis where many different types of life are disappearing. Many scientists believe this is the sixth time in Earth's history that large numbers of species have died out at once. Plants and animals all over the world face a higher risk of extinction than before. The United States has made protecting and helping endangered species a priority by passing the Endangered Species Act of nineteen seventy-three and other important conservation laws. Conservation and protection efforts under the Endangered Species Act have successfully prevented ninety-nine percent of species listed as threatened or endangered from going extinct. This includes the American bald eagle, loggerhead sea turtle, California condor, gray whale, whooping crane, Florida manatee, black-footed ferret, and Atlantic sturgeon. Many at-risk species are very important to Tribal, indigenous, and Native Alaskan communities for their culture and survival. These communities' leadership, ancestral lands, and traditional knowledge about nature play a valuable role in helping species survive and recover. Protecting species has helped the economy grow through wildlife tourism and outdoor recreation, which supports millions of American jobs. The recovery of species under the Endangered Species Act is a remarkable success that should be celebrated. Therefore, the House of Representatives supports designating Endangered Species Day, recognizes the important value of plants and wildlife as part of America's environmental heritage, and acknowledges how valuable the Endangered Species Act has been in protecting at-risk species. The House also acknowledges the continued importance of the Endangered Species Act and other conservation measures to protect threatened and endangered species and address the crisis of disappearing biodiversity.
